distribution_analysis.most_covered_section: 3.1 Fluid Statics, 3.2 Pressure in fluids at rest
distribution_analysis.least_covered_section: 3.4 Fluid flow, 3.5 Safety and high pressure

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subsection: Properties of solids, liquids and gases
questions.structural_anchor: Properties of solids, liquids and gases
questions.question: What property of gases allows them to be easily compressed compared to liquids and solids?

options.A: Large distance between particles with weak intermolecular forces
options.B: Strong intermolecular forces holding particles in fixed positions
options.C: Particles arranged in a rigid crystalline lattice structure
options.D: High density with particles in close contact but able to slide

questions.hint: Consider how particle spacing and forces affect compressibility.
questions.explanation: Gases are easily compressed because their particles are far apart with very weak intermolecular forces, allowing them to be pushed closer together. Option B describes solids, where strong forces and fixed positions resist compression.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Properties of solids, liquids and gases
source_trace.structural_element: Properties of solids, liquids and gases
source_trace.content_anchor: Atoms in gases are separated by distances that are large compared with the size of the atoms

explanations_tiered.definition: Compressibility is the ability of a substance to decrease in volume under applied pressure, determined by particle spacing and intermolecular forces.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Gases have large interparticle distances and weak forces, making them highly compressible. Liquids and solids resist compression because particles are already close together with stronger forces.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of a gas like a crowd of people with lots of empty space between them — you can squeeze them closer. Liquids and solids are like a full subway car with no extra room.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Gas particles are far apart with negligible intermolecular forces, so applying pressure forces them into a smaller volume. In contrast, liquids and solids have particles already in close contact, strongly resisting further compression.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The isothermal compressibility \(\kappa = -\frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T\) is large for gases because particle spacing allows significant volume reduction. For liquids and solids, intermolecular repulsion at short distances makes \(\kappa\) very small.
reasoning_steps: Recall the definition of compressibility.
Compare particle spacing in gases vs. liquids/solids.
Note that weak forces in gases allow volume reduction.
misconceptions: Some believe gases are compressible because particles are smaller; actually it is spacing and weak forces that matter.
Compressibility is confused with fluidity — gases compress easily but are also fluids like liquids.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Gases: large spacing, weak forces → easily compressed.

glossary_entry.definition: The ability of a substance to reduce its volume when subjected to pressure.

flashcard.front: Why are gases more compressible than liquids or solids?
flashcard.back: Gas particles are far apart with weak intermolecular forces, allowing them to be pushed closer together under pressure.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What property of gases allows easy compression?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Large interparticle distance and weak intermolecular forces.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The ability of a substance to decrease in volume under applied pressure due to particle spacing and intermolecular forces.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Gas: large spacing, weak forces → compressible.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Students often think compressibility is unique to gases; point out that all matter compresses slightly but gases do so dramatically.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the properties of solids, liquids and gases
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Gases have large interparticle distances and weak intermolecular forces, unlike solids (strong forces, fixed positions) or liquids (close contact, sliding).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A force of \(250\,\text{N}\) is applied perpendicularly to a surface of area \(0.05\,\text{m}^2\). What is the pressure on that surface in pascals?
questions.hint: Use \(P = F/A\). Divide force by area.
questions.explanation: Pressure is force per unit area: \(P = F/A = 250\,\text{N} / 0.05\,\text{m}^2 = 5000\,\text{Pa} = 5.0 \times 10^4\,\text{Pa}\). Option A incorrectly moves the decimal, while Option B uses \(F \times A\) instead of division.

explanations_tiered.definition: Pressure is the perpendicular force acting on a surface divided by the area over which the force is distributed.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The pressure equation \(P = F/A\) shows that for a given force, smaller area yields greater pressure. This principle explains why sharp objects cut easily.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine pressing a thumbtack: the same force on the pin’s tiny tip creates huge pressure, but on your thumb it’s spread out so pressure is low.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Using \(P = F/A\) with \(F = 250\,\text{N}\) and \(A = 0.05\,\text{m}^2\) gives \(P = 250 / 0.05 = 5000\,\text{Pa}\). Option C is correct because \(5000\,\text{Pa} = 5.0 \times 10^4\,\text{Pa}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Pressure is a scalar defined as \(P = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F_\perp}{\Delta A}\). For uniform force distribution, \(P = F/A\). Units: \(1\,\text{Pa} = 1\,\text{N/m}^2\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Write the pressure formula: \(P = F/A\).
reasoning_steps: Identify the formula \(P = F/A\).
Insert the given force and area.
Perform the division to find pressure in pascals.
misconceptions: Some students think pressure = force × area; this gives much larger numbers and is dimensionally incorrect.
Pressure is often confused with force — pressure is force per area, not force itself.

glossary_entry.definition: The perpendicular force per unit area acting on a surface.

flashcard.front: What is the pressure when 250 N acts on 0.05 m²?

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Perpendicular force per unit area, measured in pascals (N/m²).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that pressure is scalar and area must be in m² for Pa.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Define pressure and solve problems using P = F/A
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. \(P = 250/0.05 = 5000\,\text{Pa} = 5.0 \times 10^4\,\text{Pa}\). Option A gives 250×0.05, a common error.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: What is the term for the actual pressure at a given position measured relative to absolute vacuum?
questions.hint: This pressure includes the effect of the atmosphere above.
questions.explanation: Absolute pressure is the true pressure relative to absolute zero pressure (perfect vacuum). Gauge pressure, option A, is measured relative to atmospheric pressure, not absolute vacuum.

source_trace.content_anchor: absolute pressure is measured relative to absolute vacuum

explanations_tiered.definition: Absolute pressure is the total pressure exerted by a fluid, measured relative to a perfect vacuum (zero pressure).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Absolute pressure includes atmospheric pressure plus any gauge pressure. It is the true thermodynamic pressure used in the ideal gas law.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of absolute pressure as the total push from all molecules, including the air above you. Gauge pressure ignores the air we breathe.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Absolute pressure \(P_{\text{abs}}\) is measured from absolute zero. Gauge pressure \(P_{\text{gauge}} = P_{\text{abs}} - P_{\text{atm}}\). Tire gauges read gauge pressure.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The ideal gas law \(PV = nRT\) requires absolute pressure. At sea level, \(P_{\text{abs}} = 101.3\,\text{kPa}\) plus any additional pressure from a pump or depth.
reasoning_steps: Recall that absolute means from zero (vacuum).
Distinguish from gauge (relative to atmosphere).
Identify the definition.
misconceptions: Gauge pressure is often mistaken for absolute pressure because most common gauges show gauge pressure.
Some think absolute pressure is always larger than atmospheric; it is, except in vacuum where it is smaller.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Absolute pressure = total pressure from vacuum.

glossary_entry.definition: Pressure measured relative to a perfect vacuum.

flashcard.back: Pressure measured relative to absolute zero pressure (vacuum).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Absolute pressure is the true pressure measured relative to a perfect vacuum.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Pressure measured relative to absolute zero pressure.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Absolute: from vacuum. Gauge: from atmosphere.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use a tire gauge example: 32 psi gauge = 32 + 14.7 = 46.7 psi absolute.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Define absolute, atmospheric and gauge pressure
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Absolute pressure references absolute vacuum; gauge pressure references atmospheric pressure.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A rectangular block of metal has dimensions \(0.20\,\text{m} \times 0.10\,\text{m} \times 0.05\,\text{m}\) and a mass of \(7.8\,\text{kg}\). What is its density?
questions.hint: Density = mass / volume. First find volume = length × width × height.
questions.explanation: Volume = \(0.20 \times 0.10 \times 0.05 = 0.0010\,\text{m}^3\). Density = \(7.8 / 0.0010 = 7800\,\text{kg/m}^3 = 7.8 \times 10^3\,\text{kg/m}^3\). Option A forgets to divide by volume; Option B uses an incorrect volume.

explanations_tiered.definition: Density is the mass per unit volume of a substance, indicating how much matter is packed into a given space.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Density \(\rho = m/V\) allows identification of materials and explains buoyancy. Higher density substances sink in lower density fluids.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine two boxes the same size: one full of bricks (high density) and one full of feathers (low density). The brick box weighs much more.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: First calculate volume \(V = 0.20 \times 0.10 \times 0.05 = 0.0010\,\text{m}^3\). Then \(\rho = 7.8/0.0010 = 7800\,\text{kg/m}^3\). Iron has density around 7800 kg/m³.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Density is an intensive property. For a homogeneous object, \(\rho = \lim_{\Delta V \to 0} \frac{\Delta m}{\Delta V}\). The SI unit is kg/m³; water is 1000 kg/m³.
reasoning_steps: Calculate the volume of the rectangular block.
Use the definition of density: mass divided by volume.
Express result in scientific notation.
misconceptions: Density and mass are often confused; density is mass per volume, not total mass.
Some think density changes with sample size; it does not for a pure substance.

glossary_entry.definition: Mass per unit volume of a substance.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Density of a 7.8 kg block (0.20×0.10×0.05 m³)?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Mass per unit volume, measured in kg/m³.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Many students forget to compute volume first; emphasize step order.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Solve problems related to pressure, density and specific gravity
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Volume = 0.0010 m³, density = 7.8/0.0010 = 7800 kg/m³ = 7.8×10³ kg/m³.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: What does Pascal's principle state about a change in pressure applied to an enclosed static fluid?

options.A: It is transmitted undiminished to every point of the fluid and to the container walls
options.B: It is absorbed by the fluid and converted into thermal energy
options.C: It decreases proportionally with distance from the point of application
options.D: It only affects the bottom of the container, not the sides

questions.hint: Think about hydraulic systems like car brakes or lifts.
questions.explanation: Pascal's principle states that pressure applied to an enclosed static fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished throughout the fluid. Options B, C, and D contradict the principle; pressure does not diminish with distance in a static fluid.

source_trace.content_anchor: a change in the pressure applied to a static fluid is transmitted undiminished to every point of the fluid and to the walls of the container

explanations_tiered.definition: Pascal's principle: Pressure applied to an enclosed static fluid is transmitted undiminished to all portions of the fluid and to the container walls.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: This principle underlies hydraulic systems: a small force on a small area creates a pressure that multiplies into a large force on a larger area.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine squeezing a sealed toothpaste tube: the pressure you apply at the back spreads instantly to every part of the paste, pushing it out the nozzle.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Pascal's principle \( \Delta P \) is constant throughout. In a hydraulic press, \( F_2 = (A_2/A_1) F_1 \) because pressure is the same at both pistons.
explanations_tiered.advanced: For an incompressible fluid at rest, the pressure change \( \Delta P \) propagates instantaneously. From hydrostatics, \( P_2 = P_1 + \rho g h \), but \( \Delta P \) adds equally everywhere.
reasoning_steps: Recall the definition of Pascal's principle.
Eliminate options suggesting attenuation or directional limits.
Select the complete statement of undiminished transmission.
misconceptions: Pressure does not diminish with distance in a static enclosed fluid — this is unique to fluids, unlike sound or light.
Some think pressure only acts downward; Pascal's principle shows it acts in all directions.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Pascal: Pressure change transmitted equally throughout.

glossary_entry.definition: A change in pressure applied to an enclosed static fluid is transmitted undiminished to all parts of the fluid and container walls.

flashcard.back: Pressure applied to an enclosed static fluid is transmitted undiminished to every point of the fluid and container walls.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Pressure applied to an enclosed static fluid is transmitted undiminished throughout.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Pressure applied to an enclosed static fluid is transmitted undiminished to all points.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Demonstrate with a hydraulic jack to show force multiplication.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Pascal's principle: pressure change is transmitted undiminished throughout the fluid.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A hydraulic lift has a small piston area of \(0.0040\,\text{m}^2\) and a large piston area of \(0.20\,\text{m}^2\). What force on the small piston is required to lift a \(1200\,\text{kg}\) car on the large piston?
questions.explanation: Weight of car \(F_2 = 1200 \times 9.8 = 11760\,\text{N}\). Pascal's principle: \(F_1/A_1 = F_2/A_2\) → \(F_1 = F_2 \times (A_1/A_2) = 11760 \times (0.0040/0.20) = 11760 \times 0.02 = 235\,\text{N}\). Option A uses \(F_2/A_1\) incorrectly; Option C uses wrong area ratio.

explanations_tiered.definition: A hydraulic press uses Pascal's principle to multiply force: \(F_2 = (A_2/A_1)F_1\), where \(A_2 > A_1\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Because pressure is equal on both pistons, a small force on the small piston produces a larger force on the large piston, proportional to the area ratio.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of a bike pump connected to a wide car jack. A little push on the narrow pump creates a big lift on the wide jack because the pressure spreads out.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Hydraulic systems conserve energy: \(F_1 d_1 = F_2 d_2\) because work input = work output (ignoring friction). The area ratio directly gives force multiplication: \(F_2/F_1 = A_2/A_1\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Compute weight of car: \(F_2 = mg = 1200 \times 9.8 = 11760\,\text{N}\).
reasoning_steps: Calculate the weight of the car using \(F = mg\).
Set the pressure equal on both pistons.
Solve for the unknown force on the small piston.
misconceptions: Students sometimes think the force ratio equals area ratio directly as \(F_2 = (A_2/A_1)F_1\), which is correct, but they invert it incorrectly.
Some forget that the force on the large piston equals the weight of the load, not the mass.

glossary_entry.definition: A device using Pascal's principle to multiply force, where a small force on a small piston creates a large force on a larger piston.

flashcard.front: Calculate force needed on small piston (0.004 m²) to lift 1200 kg car on large piston (0.20 m²).

visual_system.visual_description: Two pistons connected by a fluid-filled tube. Small piston on left has area 0.0040 m², large piston on right has area 0.20 m² with a car above it.
visual_system.caption: A hydraulic lift multiplies force by area ratio.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Diagram of hydraulic lift: small piston on left, large piston on right with car, fluid connecting them.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Find force on small piston (0.004 m²) to lift 1200 kg car on large piston (0.20 m²).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A force-multiplying device using Pascal's principle.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Have students calculate the distance the small piston must move if the large piston rises 0.10 m.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain how Pascal's principle is used in hydraulic press machine
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Weight = 11760 N, force ratio = area ratio = 0.004/0.20 = 0.02, so F₁ = 11760×0.02 = 235 N.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A scuba diver descends from the surface to a depth of \(15\,\text{m}\) in fresh water (\(\rho = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3\)). By how much does the absolute pressure on the diver increase?
questions.hint: Pressure increase = \(\rho g h\). Add atmospheric pressure only if asked for absolute pressure.
questions.explanation: The increase in pressure due to depth is \( \Delta P = \rho g h = 1000 \times 9.8 \times 15 = 1.47 \times 10^5\,\text{Pa}\). Option A forgets \(g\); Option C doubles the value; Option D is atmospheric pressure alone.

explanations_tiered.definition: Pressure in a fluid increases with depth according to \(P = P_0 + \rho g h\), where \(P_0\) is pressure at the surface.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The additional pressure due to the fluid’s weight is \(\rho g h\). This is why dams are thicker at the bottom.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Diving deeper feels like more weight on your ears. Every 10 meters of water adds about 1 atmosphere of pressure (≈ 100 kPa).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Use \(\Delta P = \rho g h\). For fresh water: \(\Delta P = 1000 \times 9.8 \times 15 = 147000\,\text{Pa} = 1.47 \times 10^5\,\text{Pa}\). This is the gauge pressure increase.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Hydrostatic pressure derivation: consider a fluid column of height \(h\), weight \(\rho g h A\), pressure \(P = F/A = \rho g h\). Total absolute pressure includes atmospheric: \(P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{atm}} + \rho g h\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Identify formula for pressure increase: \(\Delta P = \rho g h\).
reasoning_steps: Recall that pressure increase with depth is \(\rho g h\).
Multiply density × gravity × depth.
Express the result in scientific notation.
misconceptions: Confusing gauge pressure increase with absolute pressure: the question asks for increase, so \(P_0\) is not added.
Forgetting to include \(g\) in the calculation (using \(\rho h\) alone).

glossary_entry.definition: The pressure due to the weight of a fluid above a given depth.

flashcard.front: Pressure increase at 15 m depth in fresh water?

visual_system.visual_title: Pressure vs Depth in Fresh Water
visual_system.visual_description: Graph showing linear increase of pressure with depth: slope = ρg = 9800 Pa/m.
visual_system.caption: Pressure increases linearly with depth in a uniform fluid.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Line graph: depth (m) on x-axis, pressure (kPa) on y-axis; straight line from (0,101.3) to (15,248)

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Pressure increase at 15 m depth, fresh water?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Pressure due to fluid weight: ρgh.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Ask students: If absolute pressure is needed, add 101.3 kPa.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Discuss how pressure depends on depth of fluid

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: What does a barometer directly measure by balancing the weight of a fluid column against atmospheric pressure?
questions.hint: Torricelli’s mercury barometer is the classic example.
questions.explanation: A barometer measures atmospheric pressure by balancing it with the hydrostatic pressure \(P = \rho g h\) of a fluid column (usually mercury). Option A is measured by a manometer, not a barometer.

explanations_tiered.definition: A barometer is an instrument that measures atmospheric pressure by balancing it against the hydrostatic pressure of a liquid column.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Torricelli’s barometer uses mercury: the height of the mercury column (≈760 mm at sea level) indicates atmospheric pressure.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A barometer is like a straw stuck into a cup of mercury, but the top is sealed. The air pushes down on the mercury in the cup, forcing it up the tube.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In a barometer, \(P_{\text{atm}} = \rho g h\). At sea level with mercury, \(h \approx 0.760\,\text{m}\). If water were used, \(h \approx 10.3\,\text{m}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The barometer equation \(P_{\text{atm}} = \rho_{\text{Hg}} g h\) assumes negligible vapor pressure above the mercury column. Standard atmosphere: 760 mmHg = 101.325 kPa.
reasoning_steps: Recall the purpose of a barometer.
Know that it balances atmospheric pressure with a fluid column.
Select the correct measured quantity.
misconceptions: Barometer measures gauge pressure? No, it measures absolute atmospheric pressure.
The height depends on tube diameter? No, only on density, gravity, and atmospheric pressure.

glossary_entry.definition: A device that measures atmospheric pressure using a fluid column.

flashcard.back: Atmospheric pressure, using a fluid column balanced against the air.

visual_system.visual_description: A glass tube inverted in a mercury reservoir; mercury column height around 760 mm at sea level.
visual_system.caption: A mercury barometer balances atmospheric pressure with a mercury column.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Diagram of barometer: vertical tube with mercury, height h, open reservoir at bottom.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Instrument measuring atmospheric pressure via a fluid column.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show that barometer height changes with altitude.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Discuss the working principles of barometers
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. A barometer directly measures atmospheric pressure using Patm = ρgh.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Why does a submerged object experience a net upward buoyant force?

options.A: The pressure at the top of the object is greater than at the bottom
options.B: The object is less dense than the fluid
options.C: Pressure increases with depth, so upward force on bottom exceeds downward force on top
options.D: The fluid pushes sideways, creating lift

questions.hint: Consider how pressure changes with depth in a fluid.
questions.explanation: Buoyant force arises because pressure increases with depth (\(P = P_0 + \rho g h\)). The upward force on the bottom face (\(PA\) with larger \(P\)) is greater than the downward force on the top face. Option A reverses the pressure comparison; Option B describes floating, not the cause of buoyancy.
formula_used: \(F_B = P_{\text{bottom}}A - P_{\text{top}}A = \rho g h A = \rho g V_{\text{disp}}\)

source_trace.content_anchor: pressure increases with depth … upward force on the bottom … greater than downward force on the top

explanations_tiered.definition: Buoyant force is the net upward force exerted by a fluid on an object due to the increase of pressure with depth.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Pressure increases linearly with depth, so the upward force on the bottom of an object is larger than the downward force on the top, producing a net upward force.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine being underwater: your feet feel more pressure than your head because water is heavier deeper down. That extra push on your feet gives you a lifting feeling.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: At depth, \(P = P_0 + \rho g h\). For a submerged object, \(P_{\text{bottom}} > P_{\text{top}}\), so \(F_{\text{up}} = P_{\text{bottom}}A\) > \(F_{\text{down}} = P_{\text{top}}A\). The net upward force is the buoyant force.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Derivation: For a cylinder of height \(h\) and area \(A\), \(F_B = (P_0 + \rho g h_{\text{bottom}})A - (P_0 + \rho g h_{\text{top}})A = \rho g (h_{\text{bottom}} - h_{\text{top}})A = \rho g h A = \rho g V_{\text{disp}}\).
reasoning_steps: Recall that pressure increases with depth.
Compare upward force on bottom vs downward force on top.
Conclude that the pressure difference causes net upward force.
misconceptions: Buoyancy is not due to the object’s density alone; density determines whether it floats, but the force exists regardless.
Some think buoyancy is caused by surface tension or air bubbles; it is purely hydrostatic pressure difference.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Buoyancy: Pbottom > Ptop → net upward force.

glossary_entry.definition: The net upward force exerted by a fluid on a submerged object due to pressure increasing with depth.

flashcard.front: Why is there a net upward buoyant force on a submerged object?
flashcard.back: Pressure increases with depth, so upward force on bottom exceeds downward force on top.

visual_system.visual_description: A rectangular block submerged in fluid; arrows: longer upward arrow on bottom, shorter downward arrow on top.
visual_system.caption: Pressure increases with depth, producing a net upward buoyant force.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Diagram of submerged block with larger upward arrow at bottom than downward arrow at top.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Pressure increases with depth, so upward force on bottom > downward force on top.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Net upward force from fluid pressure increasing with depth.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Buoyancy: deeper = higher pressure → upward net force.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use a force diagram: two horizontal forces cancel, vertical forces do not.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Pressure increases with depth, so Pbottom > Ptop, giving net upward force.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A wooden block floats in water with \(75\%\) of its volume submerged. What is the density of the wood?
questions.explanation: Archimedes’ principle for floating: \(\rho_{\text{obj}}/\rho_{\text{fluid}} = V_{\text{sub}}/V_{\text{obj}} = 0.75\). So \(\rho_{\text{wood}} = 0.75 \times 1000 = 750\,\text{kg/m}^3\). Option A uses 25% (wrong fraction); Option C equals water density (would float fully submerged).

explanations_tiered.definition: For a floating object in equilibrium, the weight equals the buoyant force, giving \( \rho_{\text{obj}}/\rho_{\text{fluid}} = V_{\text{sub}}/V_{\text{obj}}\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The fraction of volume submerged equals the ratio of the object’s density to the fluid’s density. Less dense objects float higher.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A beach ball floats high because its density is much less than water. A wooden block that is 75% submerged has density 0.75 times that of water.
explanations_tiered.advanced: From equilibrium: \( \rho_{\text{obj}} V_{\text{obj}} g = \rho_{\text{fluid}} V_{\text{sub}} g\). Cancel \(g\) → \( \rho_{\text{obj}}/\rho_{\text{fluid}} = V_{\text{sub}}/V_{\text{obj}}\). The submerged fraction directly gives density ratio.
reasoning_steps: Recognize that for floating, weight = buoyant force.
Express both in terms of density and volume.
Solve for the unknown density.
misconceptions: Some think 75% submerged means object density is 25% of water (that would be 25% submerged).
Confusing submerged fraction with density ratio inverted.

glossary_entry.definition: A condition where an object rests on a fluid surface, partially submerged, with weight balanced by buoyant force.

flashcard.front: Wood floats with 75% submerged. Find wood density (water = 1000 kg/m³).

visual_system.visual_description: A rectangular block floating in water; 75% of its volume below waterline, 25% above.
visual_system.caption: A floating object displaces a volume of fluid equal to its weight.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Diagram: wood block floating, 3/4 underwater, 1/4 above water.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: 75% submerged wood in water. Density of wood?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Object partially submerged, weight balanced by buoyant force.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Ask: What would fraction be if wood density were 500 kg/m³? (50%).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Solve problems related to buoyant force
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Vsub/Vobj = ρwood/ρwater → ρwood = 0.75 × 1000 = 750 kg/m³.
